1.o: All Christians must be saints; if they come not under that character on earth, they will never be saints in glory. Those are not saints, who are not faithful, believing in Christ, and true to the profession they make of relation to their Lord. By grace, understand the free and undeserved love and favour of God, and those graces of the Spirit which come from it; by peace, all other blessings, spiritual and temporal, the fruits of the former. No peace without grace. No peace, nor grace, but from God the Father, and from the Lord Jesus Christ; and the best saints need fresh supplies of the graces of the Spirit, and desire to grow.
1.m:
All Christians should be saints. If they aren't saints on Earth, they won’t be saints in heaven either. People who aren’t faithful and don’t believe in Christ aren’t considered saints. 

Grace means the free and undeserved love and favor from God, along with the spiritual gifts that come from him. Peace includes all other blessings, both spiritual and physical, that result from having grace. There’s no peace without grace. Grace and peace only come from God the Father and from Jesus Christ. Even the best saints need new supplies of God’s grace and want to keep growing spiritually.

1.o: Spiritual and heavenly blessings are the best blessings; with which we cannot be miserable, and without which we cannot but be so. This was from the choice of them in Christ, before the foundation of the world, that they should be made holy by separation from sin, being set apart to God, and sanctified by the Holy Spirit, in consequence of their election in Christ. All who are chosen to happiness as the end, are chosen to holiness as the means. In love they were predestinated, or fore-ordained, to be adopted as children of God by faith in Christ Jesus, and to be openly admitted to the privileges of that high relation to himself. The reconciled and adopted believer, the pardoned sinner, gives all the praise of his salvation to his gracious Father. His love appointed this method of redemption, spared not his own Son, and brought believers to hear and embrace this salvation. It was rich grace to provide such a surety as his own Son, and freely to deliver him up. This method of grace gives no encouragement to evil, but shows sin in all its hatefulness, and how it deserves vengeance. The believer's actions, as well as his words, declare the praises of Divine mercy.
1.m:
The best blessings are spiritual ones. With these blessings, we cannot be unhappy, and without them, we cannot be happy. God chose people for these blessings through Jesus even before the world began. He picked them to be separate from sin, setting them apart for God and making them holy through the Holy Spirit. 

Those chosen for happiness are also chosen for holiness. They were loved and chosen ahead of time to become God’s children through faith in Jesus. They will be openly accepted as part of God’s family. A forgiven person gives all the credit for their salvation to God. God planned this way of saving us, did not hold back his own Son, and led believers to accept this gift. 

It was very kind of God to offer such a perfect helper as his own Son and to freely give him over. This plan teaches us that sin is terrible and deserves punishment. But it also lets us see God’s great mercy. Our actions and words show how thankful we are for God’s kindness.

1.o: Blessings were made known to believers, by the Lord's showing to them the mystery of his sovereign will, and the method of redemption and salvation. But these must have been for ever hidden from us, if God had not made them known by his written word, preached gospel, and Spirit of truth. Christ united the two differing parties, God and man, in his own person, and satisfied for that wrong which caused the separation. He wrought, by his Spirit, those graces of faith and love, whereby we are made one with God, and among ourselves. He dispenses all his blessings, according to his good pleasure. His Divine teaching led whom he pleased to see the glory of those truths, which others were left to blaspheme. What a gracious promise that is, which secures the gift of the Holy Ghost to those who ask him! The sanctifying and comforting influences of the Holy Spirit seal believers as the children of God, and heirs of heaven. These are the first-fruits of holy happiness. For this we were made, and for this we were redeemed; this is the great design of God in all that he has done for us; let all be ascribed unto the praise of his glory.
1.m:
God showed believers the plan of his will and the way to be saved through Jesus. Without God’s Word, preaching, and the Holy Spirit, we would never know these things. 

Christ joined God and humans together by paying for our sins. Through his Spirit, he gives us faith and love so we can become one with God and each other. 

He shares his gifts based on what he wants. Some people see the beauty of these truths because of God’s guidance, while others do not. 

It’s wonderful that God promises to give the Holy Spirit to those who ask. This Spirit makes believers feel clean and happy, proving they belong to God and will go to heaven. 

These blessings are the start of eternal joy. We were created and saved for this purpose. Everything God does points to his greatness and glory.

1.o: God has laid up spiritual blessings for us in his Son the Lord Jesus; but requires us to draw them out and fetch them in by prayer. Even the best Christians need to be prayed for: and while we hear of the welfare of Christian friends, we should pray for them. Even true believers greatly want heavenly wisdom. Are not the best of us unwilling to come under God's yoke, though there is no other way to find rest for the soul? Do we not for a little pleasure often part with our peace? And if we dispute less, and prayed more with and for each other, we should daily see more and more what is the hope of our calling, and the riches of the Divine glory in this inheritance. It is desirable to feel the mighty power of Divine grace, beginning and carrying on the work of faith in our souls. But it is difficult to bring a soul to believe fully in Christ, and to venture its all, and the hope of eternal life, upon his righteousness. Nothing less than Almighty power will work this in us. Here is signified that it is Christ the Saviour, who supplies all the necessities of those who trust in him, and gives them all blessings in the richest abundance. And by being partakers of Christ himself, we come to be filled with the fulness of grace and glory in him. How then do those forget themselves who seek for righteousness out of him! This teaches us to come to Christ. And did we know what we are called to, and what we might find in him, surely we should come and be suitors to him. When feeling our weakness and the power of our enemies, we most perceive the greatness of that mighty power which effects the conversion of the believer, and is engaged to perfect his salvation. Surely this will constrain us by love to live to our Redeemer's glory.
1.m:
God has stored up spiritual blessings for us in his Son, Jesus. We need to ask for these blessings through prayer. Even the best Christians still need people to pray for them. So when you hear about your Christian friends doing well, remember to pray for them too.

True believers often lack heavenly wisdom. The best among us sometimes avoid following God’s ways because we don’t want to give up small pleasures. If we argued less and prayed more together, we would see clearer what our hopes are and learn more about God’s glory.

It’s good to experience God’s powerful grace at work in our lives. But believing fully in Christ and trusting everything to his righteousness is hard. Only God’s great power can help us do this.

Christ provides everything we need when we put our trust in him. He gives us all kinds of blessings in full measure. By sharing in Christ, we receive all the grace and glory he offers.

Those who look elsewhere for their righteousness are forgetting the source. Knowing what we gain from Christ should motivate us to turn to him. Feeling weak and seeing the strength of our enemies makes us realize the power needed to save us. This understanding should move us to live for Christ’s honor.

2.o: Sin is the death of the soul. A man dead in trespasses and sins has no desire for spiritual pleasures. When we look upon a corpse, it gives an awful feeling. A never-dying spirit is now fled, and has left nothing but the ruins of a man. But if we viewed things aright, we should be far more affected by the thought of a dead soul, a lost, fallen spirit. A state of sin is a state of conformity to this world. Wicked men are slaves to Satan. Satan is the author of that proud, carnal disposition which there is in ungodly men; he rules in the hearts of men. From Scripture it is clear, that whether men have been most prone to sensual or to spiritual wickedness, all men, being naturally children of disobedience, are also by nature children of wrath. What reason have sinners, then, to seek earnestly for that grace which will make them, of children of wrath, children of God and heirs of glory! God's eternal love or good-will toward his creatures, is the fountain whence all his mercies flow to us; and that love of God is great love, and that mercy is rich mercy. And every converted sinner is a saved sinner; delivered from sin and wrath. The grace that saves is the free, undeserved goodness and favour of God; and he saves, not by the works of the law, but through faith in Christ Jesus. Grace in the soul is a new life in the soul. A regenerated sinner becomes a living soul; he lives a life of holiness, being born of God: he lives, being delivered from the guilt of sin, by pardoning and justifying grace. Sinners roll themselves in the dust; sanctified souls sit in heavenly places, are raised above this world, by Christ's grace. The goodness of God in converting and saving sinners heretofore, encourages others in after-time, to hope in his grace and mercy. Our faith, our conversion, and our eternal salvation, are not of works, lest any man should boast. These things are not brought to pass by any thing done by us, therefore all boasting is shut out. All is the free gift of God, and the effect of being quickened by his power. It was his purpose, to which he prepared us, by blessing us with the knowledge of his will, and his Holy Spirit producing such a change in us, that we should glorify God by our good conversation, and perseverance in holiness. None can from Scripture abuse this doctrine, or accuse it of any tendency to evil. All who do so, are without excuse.
2.m:
Sin kills the soul. Someone who is dead in their sins doesn’t want anything spiritual. Looking at a dead body makes you feel bad because a never-dying spirit has gone away, leaving behind only the body’s remains. But thinking about a soul that is spiritually dead should affect us even more. 

When people live in sin, they follow the ways of this world. They become slaves to Satan. He controls those who are proud and selfish. Whether someone likes physical pleasure or mental pride, everyone is naturally sinful and deserves punishment. So, sinners need to urgently seek God’s grace to turn them from children of punishment into children of God and heirs of heaven.

God’s love for us is the source of all His kindness. This love is very strong. When God shows mercy, it is truly generous. Each person who turns to God is saved from sin and punishment. The grace that saves comes freely from God, not because of what we do. It comes through believing in Jesus.

Grace changes the soul. A person who is reborn becomes alive again. They start living a holy life because God forgives them. Sinners used to wallow in dirt, but now, thanks to Christ, they are lifted up to heaven. People who were saved before encourage others to trust in God’s grace too.

Our faith, our turning to God, and our future salvation don’t depend on our actions. We can’t take credit for these things. Everything is a free gift from God, given by His powerful work in us.

His goal was to prepare us by giving us knowledge about his will and changing us through his Holy Spirit. This way, we would honor God through our good actions and continue living holy lives. No one can misuse this teaching or claim it leads to anything bad. Those who do are wrong and have no excuse.

2.o: Christ and his covenant are the foundation of all the Christian's hopes. A sad and terrible description is here; but who is able to remove himself out of it? Would that this were not a true description of many baptized in the name of Christ. Who can, without trembling, reflect upon the misery of a person, separated for ever from the people of God, cut off from the body of Christ, fallen from the covenant of promise, having no hope, no Saviour, and without any God but a God of vengeance, to all eternity? To have no part in Christ! What true Christian can hear this without horror? Salvation is far from the wicked; but God is a help at hand to his people; and this is by the sufferings and death of Christ.
2.m: Christ and his covenant are the foundation of all a Christian’s hopes. Here we see a sad and scary picture. But who can get away from it? Sadly, this describes many who were baptized in the name of Christ. Who can think about being forever separated from God’s people, cut off from Jesus’ followers, and losing their place in God’s promises without feeling scared? To be without any share in Christ—what true believer wouldn’t feel horrified hearing this? The wicked are far from salvation, but God helps those who belong to him through what Christ suffered and died for.

2.o: Jesus Christ made peace by the sacrifice of himself; in every sense Christ was their Peace, the author, centre, and substance of their being at peace with God, and of their union with the Jewish believers in one church. Through the person, sacrifice, and mediation of Christ, sinners are allowed to draw near to God as a Father, and are brought with acceptance into his presence, with their worship and services, under the teaching of the Holy Spirit, as one with the Father and the Son. Christ purchased leave for us to come to God; and the Spirit gives a heart to come, and strength to come, and then grace to serve God acceptably.
2.m:
Jesus Christ made peace through his sacrifice. In every way, he was their Peace—making them at peace with God and joining Jewish believers together in one church. 

Through Jesus, people can now approach God like a loving Father. They can bring their worship and offerings to him, guided by the Holy Spirit. This makes them one with both the Father and the Son.

Christ paid the price so we could come to God. The Holy Spirit gives us the desire to come, the strength to do it, and the ability to serve God well.

2.o: The church is compared to a city, and every converted sinner is free of it. It is also compared to a house, and every converted sinner is one of the family; a servant, and a child in God's house. The church is also compared to a building, founded on the doctrine of Christ; delivered by the prophets of the Old Testament, and the apostles of the New. God dwells in all believers now; they become the temple of God through the working of the blessed Spirit. Let us then ask if our hopes are fixed on Christ, according to the doctrine of his word? Have we devoted ourselves as holy temples to God through him? Are we habitations of God by the Spirit, are we spiritually-minded, and do we bring forth the fruits of the Spirit? Let us take heed not to grieve the holy Comforter. Let us desire his gracious presence, and his influences upon our hearts. Let us seek to discharge the duties allotted to us, to the glory of God.
2.m:
The church is like a city where anyone who turns to God can live freely. It’s also like a home where each person who follows Jesus becomes part of the family—a servant and a child in God’s household. 

The church is also like a building built on the teachings of Christ. These teachings were given by the prophets in the Old Testament and the apostles in the New Testament. Right now, God lives inside everyone who believes. He makes them His temple through the work of the Holy Spirit.

So let’s check if we trust in Christ based on what His Word teaches. Have we dedicated ourselves as holy places for God through Him? Are we homes for God by the Spirit, thinking and acting in spiritual ways? Are we showing the good results of the Spirit?

Let’s be careful not to upset the Holy Spirit. We should want Him to bless us and guide us. And we should try to do our jobs well to honor God.

3.o: For having preached the doctrine of truth, the apostle was a prisoner, but a prisoner of Jesus Christ; the object of special protection and care, while thus suffering for him. All the gracious offers of the gospel, and the joyful tidings it contains, come from the rich grace of God; it is the great means by which the Spirit works grace in the souls of men. The mystery, is that secret, hidden purpose of salvation through Christ. This was not so fully and clearly shown in the ages before Christ, as unto the prophets of the New Testament. This was the great truth made known to the apostle, that God would call the Gentiles to salvation by faith in Christ. An effectual working of Divine power attends the gifts of Divine grace. As God appointed Paul to the office, so he qualified him for it.
3.m:
After preaching the truth, the apostle became a prisoner for Jesus Christ. He received special protection and care even while suffering for Him. 

All the good news and promises of the gospel come from God’s generous love. It helps the Holy Spirit work His goodness in people’s hearts. 

The mystery here is God’s hidden plan of saving people through Christ. Before Christ came, this wasn’t explained as clearly to the Old Testament prophets as it was to those who wrote the New Testament.

God revealed to the apostle that He would save Gentiles through their faith in Christ. When God gives us His grace, He also works powerfully within us. Just as God chose Paul for his role, He gave him the skills needed for it.

3.o: Those whom God advances to honourable employments, he makes low in their own eyes; and where God gives grace to be humble, there he gives all other needful grace. How highly he speaks of Jesus Christ; the unsearchable riches of Christ! Though many are not enriched with these riches; yet how great a favour to have them preached among us, and to have an offer of them! And if we are not enriched with them it is our own fault. The first creation, when God made all things out of nothing, and the new creation, whereby sinners are made new creatures by converting grace, are of God by Jesus Christ. His riches are as unsearchable and as sure as ever, yet while angels adore the wisdom of God in the redemption of his church, the ignorance of self-wise and carnal men deems the whole to be foolishness.
3.m:
God puts those he honors in important jobs down in their own eyes. When God gives someone grace to be humble, he also gives them all the other grace they need. Think about Jesus Christ—what amazing gifts he has! Even though some people don’t get these gifts, it’s still a big favor for them to hear about them. And if we miss out on these gifts, it’s our own fault.

The first time God created everything from nothing was amazing. But making sinners into new people through Jesus’ grace is just as amazing. Both creations come from God through Jesus. These gifts are always there, even when smart people think they’re silly. While angels praise God’s wisdom in saving his church, ignorant people see it as nonsense.

3.o: The apostle seems to be more anxious lest the believers should be discouraged and faint upon his tribulations, than for what he himself had to bear. He asks for spiritual blessings, which are the best blessings. Strength from the Spirit of God in the inner man; strength in the soul; the strength of faith, to serve God, and to do our duty. If the law of Christ is written in our hearts, and the love of Christ is shed abroad there, then Christ dwells there. Where his Spirit dwells, there he dwells. We should desire that good affections may be fixed in us. And how desirable to have a fixed sense of the love of God in Christ to our souls! How powerfully the apostle speaks of the love of Christ! The breadth shows its extent to all nations and ranks; the length, that it continues from everlasting to everlasting; the depth, its saving those who are sunk into the depths of sin and misery; the height, its raising them up to heavenly happiness and glory. Those who receive grace for grace from Christ's fulness, may be said to be filled with the fulness of God. Should not this satisfy man? Must he needs fill himself with a thousand trifles, fancying thereby to complete his happiness?
3.m:
The apostle seems more worried about the believers getting discouraged by his troubles than about his own hardships. He asks for spiritual gifts, which are the best kinds of gifts. He wants strength from God’s Spirit inside him; strength in his soul; the strength of faith to serve God and do his duties. 

If we let the teachings of Jesus guide us and feel his love in our hearts, then Jesus lives within us. When his Spirit is present, so is he. We should want good feelings to stay with us. It’s wonderful to know deeply how much Jesus loves us!

The apostle strongly talks about Jesus’ love. Its width means it reaches everyone everywhere. Its length means it lasts forever. Its depth means it saves people trapped in sin and suffering. Its height means it lifts them to heaven.

Those who get grace from Jesus' fullness can be called filled with God’s fullness. Isn’t this enough for humans? Why do they need to fill themselves with many small things, thinking that will bring true happiness?

3.o: It is proper always to end prayers with praises. Let us expect more, and ask for more, encouraged by what Christ has already done for our souls, being assured that the conversion of sinners, and the comfort of believers, will be to his glory, for ever and ever.
3.m: We should always end our prayers with praise. We can hope for more and ask for more because of what Christ has already done for us. We can be sure that helping sinners turn from their wrong ways and bringing comfort to those who believe will bring honor to him forever.

4.o: Nothing is pressed more earnestly in the Scriptures, than to walk as becomes those called to Christ's kingdom and glory. By lowliness, understand humility, which is opposed to pride. By meekness, that excellent disposition of soul, which makes men unwilling to provoke, and not easily to be provoked or offended. We find much in ourselves for which we can hardly forgive ourselves; therefore we must not be surprised if we find in others that which we think it hard to forgive. There is one Christ in whom all believers hope, and one heaven they are all hoping for; therefore they should be of one heart. They had all one faith, as to its object, Author, nature, and power. They all believed the same as to the great truths of religion; they had all been admitted into the church by one baptism, with water, in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Ghost, as the sign of regeneration. In all believers God the Father dwells, as in his holy temple, by his Spirit and special grace.
4.m:
The Bible strongly urges us to live in ways that honor our place in Christ’s kingdom and future glory.

By being humble, we mean avoiding pride. Being meek means having a gentle spirit that doesn’t get upset easily or try to start arguments.

We often struggle to forgive ourselves for our mistakes. So, we shouldn't expect to easily forgive others either.

All Christians have the same hope—Jesus—and look forward to the same reward—heaven. This shared goal should bring them together.

They believe in the same things about Jesus and were baptized in the same way, using water in the name of the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. Baptism shows that they’ve become new people.

God lives inside each believer, just like he would in a holy temple. He fills them with his Spirit and grace.

4.o: Unto every believer is given some gift of grace, for their mutual help. All is given as seems best to Christ to bestow upon every one. He received for them, that he might give to them, a large measure of gifts and graces; particularly the gift of the Holy Ghost. Not a mere head knowledge, or bare acknowledging Christ to be the Son of God, but such as brings trust and obedience. There is a fulness in Christ, and a measure of that fulness given in the counsel of God to every believer; but we never come to the perfect measure till we come to heaven. God's children are growing, as long as they are in this world; and the Christian's growth tends to the glory of Christ. The more a man finds himself drawn out to improve in his station, and according to his measure, all that he has received, to the spiritual good of others, he may the more certainly believe that he has the grace of sincere love and charity rooted in his heart. (Eph 4:17-24)
4.m:
Every believer receives a gift from God to help others. These gifts are given based on what Jesus thinks is best for each person. Jesus gave these gifts so believers could share them with others, especially the gift of the Holy Spirit. It’s not just knowing about Jesus, but trusting him and obeying him too.

There is plenty of grace in Christ, and God gives a portion of that grace to each believer. But we don’t get the full amount until we reach heaven. As long as we’re here on earth, God’s children keep growing. And the more a Christian grows, the more honor they bring to Christ.

When someone uses the gifts they have been given to help others spiritually, they can be sure that they truly love others with God’s love. (Eph 4:17-24)

4.o: The apostle charged the Ephesians in the name and by the authority of the Lord Jesus, that having professed the gospel, they should not be as the unconverted Gentiles, who walked in vain fancies and carnal affections. Do not men, on every side, walk in the vanity of their minds? Must not we then urge the distinction between real and nominal Christians? They were void of all saving knowledge; they sat in darkness, and loved it rather than light. They had a dislike and hatred to a life of holiness, which is not only the way of life God requires and approves, and by which we live to him, but which has some likeness to God himself in his purity, righteousness, truth, and goodness. The truth of Christ appears in its beauty and power, when it appears as in Jesus. The corrupt nature is called a man; like the human body, it is of divers parts, supporting and strengthening one another. Sinful desires are deceitful lusts; they promise men happiness, but render them more miserable; and bring them to destruction, if not subdued and mortified. These therefore must be put off, as an old garment, a filthy garment; they must be subdued and mortified. But it is not enough to shake off corrupt principles; we must have gracious ones. By the new man, is meant the new nature, the new creature, directed by a new principle, even regenerating grace, enabling a man to lead a new life of righteousness and holiness. This is created, or brought forth by God's almighty power.
4.m:
The apostle told the people in Ephesus, using the authority of Jesus, that after they said they believed in the gospel, they shouldn't act like those who didn’t know God. Don’t people everywhere walk around with empty ideas and selfish desires?

Shouldn’t we talk about the difference between true believers and just people who call themselves Christian? 

These non-believers lacked any real understanding of God. They lived in spiritual darkness and preferred it over the light of truth. They hated living holy lives, which please God and show His qualities of purity, justice, truth, and kindness. When we see the truth of Christ, it shines brightest through Jesus.

Their sinful natures are compared to humans because different parts work together to support sin. Their evil desires trick them into thinking they will find happiness, but instead cause misery and ruin unless stopped. So these bad habits need to be left behind, like throwing away old clothes. 

But getting rid of wrong beliefs isn’t enough. We need good ones too. A “new person” means someone changed by God’s powerful grace. This new person follows God’s ways and leads a righteous, holy life. God creates this new self within us through His mighty power.

4.o: Notice the particulars wherewith we should adorn our Christian profession. Take heed of every thing contrary to truth. No longer flatter or deceive others. God's people are children who will not lie, who dare not lie, who hate and abhor lying. Take heed of anger and ungoverned passions. If there is just occasion to express displeasure at what is wrong, and to reprove, see that it be without sin. We give place to the devil, when the first motions of sin are not grievous to our souls; when we consent to them; and when we repeat an evil deed. This teaches that as sin, if yielded unto, lets in the devil upon us, we are to resist it, keeping from all appearance of evil. Idleness makes thieves. Those who will not work, expose themselves to temptations to steal. Men ought to be industrious, that they may do some good, and that they may be kept from temptation. They must labour, not only that they may live honestly, but that they may have to give to the wants of others. What then must we think of those called Christians, who grow rich by fraud, oppression, and deceitful practices! Alms, to be accepted of God, must not be gained by unrighteousness and robbery, but by honesty and industry. God hates robbery for burnt-offerings.
4.m:
Pay attention to the details that show you’re a true follower of Christ. Be careful about anything that goes against being honest. Don’t try to fool or cheat anyone anymore. God’s people don’t tell lies because they can’t and won’t. 

Watch out for getting angry or letting your emotions get out of control. If someone needs to be corrected, do it fairly and without sin. When bad thoughts start, if we let them continue, we open the door for more sin. So we need to fight against these thoughts right away.

Idleness leads to stealing. People who refuse to work put themselves in danger of doing wrong things. Everyone should work hard so they can help others and avoid trouble. Work not only to earn money honestly but also to share with those who need it.

Think about Christians who become wealthy through cheating and dishonesty! That’s not okay. To please God, your alms giving shouldn’t come from unfair or stolen money. It should come from working hard and honestly. God doesn’t accept offerings made from robbery.

4.o: Filthy words proceed from corruption in the speaker, and they corrupt the minds and manners of those who hear them: Christians should beware of all such discourse. It is the duty of Christians to seek, by the blessing of God, to bring persons to think seriously, and to encourage and warn believers by their conversation. Be ye kind one to another. This sets forth the principle of love in the heart, and the outward expression of it, in a humble, courteous behaviour. Mark how God's forgiveness causes us to forgive. God forgives us, though we had no cause to sin against him. We must forgive, as he has forgiven us. All lying, and corrupt communications, that stir up evil desires and lusts, grieve the Spirit of God. Corrupt passions of bitterness, wrath, anger, clamour, evil-speaking, and malice, grieve the Holy Spirit. Provoke not the holy, blessed Spirit of God to withdraw his presence and his gracious influences. The body will be redeemed from the power of the grave at the resurrection day. Wherever that blessed Spirit dwells as a Sanctifier, he is the earnest of all the joys and glories of that redemption day; and we should be undone, should God take away his Holy Spirit from us.
4.m:
Dirty words come from bad thoughts inside people, and they can make others think badly too. Christians need to watch what they say. They should talk in ways that help others think carefully and support other believers. 

Be nice to each other. This shows kindness in your heart and how you act towards others. Notice how God’s forgiveness makes us forgive others. Even if we didn’t deserve it, God forgives us. So we should forgive others just like He forgives us.

Avoid telling lies and saying things that might make people do wrong things. These kinds of words upset the Holy Spirit. Bitterness, anger, yelling, mean talking, and being unkind also upset Him. Don’t make the Holy Spirit leave because of these bad feelings.

At the end of time, our bodies will be raised from the dead. When the Holy Spirit lives in someone, it means they have a promise of future blessings. Without His presence, we would lose everything good.

5.o: Because God, for Christ's sake, has forgiven you, therefore be ye followers of God, imitators of God. Resemble him especially in his love and pardoning goodness, as becomes those beloved by their heavenly Father. In Christ's sacrifice his love triumphs, and we are to consider it fully.
5.m: Since God forgave you because of Christ, follow God and copy him. Show his love and kindness, just like he did. Focus on his love, since your heavenly Father loves you. Think about how much God loves you through Christ’s sacrifice.

5.o: Filthy lusts must be rooted out. These sins must be dreaded and detested. Here are not only cautions against gross acts of sin, but against what some may make light of. But these things are so far from being profitable. that they pollute and poison the hearers. Our cheerfulness should show itself as becomes Christians, in what may tend to God's glory. A covetous man makes a god of his money; places that hope, confidence, and delight, in worldly good, which should be in God only. Those who allow themselves, either in the lusts of the flesh or the love of the world, belong not to the kingdom of grace, nor shall they come to the kingdom of glory. When the vilest transgressors repent and believe the gospel, they become children of obedience, from whom God's wrath is turned away. Dare we make light of that which brings down the wrath of God? Sinners, like men in the dark, are going they know not whither, and doing they know not what. But the grace of God wrought a mighty change in the souls of many. Walk as children of light, as having knowledge and holiness. These works of darkness are unfruitful, whatever profit they may boast; for they end in the destruction of the impenitent sinner. There are many ways of abetting, or taking part in the sins of others; by commendation, counsel, consent, or concealment. And if we share with others in their sins, we must expect to share in their plagues. If we do not reprove the sins of others, we have fellowship with them. A good man will be ashamed to speak of what many wicked men are not ashamed to do. We must have not only a sight and a knowledge that sin is sin, and in some measure shameful, but see it as a breach of God's holy law. After the example of prophets and apostles, we should call on those asleep and dead in sin, to awake and arise, that Christ may give them light.
5.m:
We need to get rid of sinful desires completely. These sins are very serious and should be feared. The passage warns us about both major sins and smaller ones that people might think don’t matter much. But all such sins harm our hearts and minds. As believers, we should show joy that honors God. Someone who loves money too much worships wealth instead of God. People who let themselves be controlled by lust or greed aren't part of God’s family now, and won't go to heaven later. Even the worst sinners can turn around and start obeying God if they truly repent and trust Jesus. Shouldn’t we take seriously anything that could bring God’s anger?

Sinners often act blindly, without knowing where they’re headed or what they’re really doing. Yet God’s kindness changed many lives powerfully. Live as people who follow the truth and walk in righteousness. Sins that hide in darkness produce nothing good. They lead to ruin for those who refuse to change. There are different ways to help others in their wrongdoings—by praising them, advising them, agreeing with them, or hiding their mistakes. By joining in other people’s sins, we’ll face similar consequences. If we don’t correct others’ faults, we’re supporting them in their wrongdoing. Good people feel embarrassed talking about sins that bad people openly commit. We must see sin as breaking God’s laws, not just as something embarrassing. Like prophets and apostles did, we should urge those trapped in sin to wake up and find new life through Christ.

5.o: Another remedy against sin, is care, or caution, it being impossible else to maintain purity of heart and life. Time is a talent given us by God, and it is misspent and lost when not employed according to his design. If we have lost our time heretofore, we must double our diligence for the future. Of that time which thousands on a dying bed would gladly redeem at the price of the whole world, how little do men think, and to what trifles they daily sacrifice it! People are very apt to complain of bad times; it were well if that stirred them more to redeem time. Be not unwise. Ignorance of our duty, and neglect of our souls, show the greatest folly. Drunkenness is a sin that never goes alone, but carries men into other evils; it is a sin very provoking to God. The drunkard holds out to his family and to the world the sad spectacle of a sinner hardened beyond what is common, and hastening to perdition. When afflicted or weary, let us not seek to raise our spirits by strong drink, which is hateful and hurtful, and only ends in making sorrows more felt. But by fervent prayer let us seek to be filled with the Spirit, and to avoid whatever may grieve our gracious Comforter. All God's people have reason to sing for joy. Though we are not always singing, we should be always giving thanks; we should never want disposition for this duty, as we never want matter for it, through the whole course of our lives. Always, even in trials and afflictions, and for all things; being satisfied of their loving intent, and good tendency. God keeps believers from sinning against him, and engages them to submit one to another in all he has commanded, to promote his glory, and to fulfil their duties to each other.
5.m:
Another way to fight sin is to be careful and cautious. It’s hard to keep our hearts and lives pure without being watchful.

God gave us time as a gift, and we waste it if we don’t use it the way He wants. If we’ve wasted time before, we need to work twice as hard now. Think about how much time people wish they could get back when they’re close to death—they’d give anything. Yet most days, people don’t think about wasting time and spend it on silly things.

People often complain about tough times, but they should instead try harder to make good use of their time.

Don't act foolishly. Being ignorant of what you should do and ignoring your soul shows great stupidity. Drinking too much leads to many other sins. It makes God angry.

Drunkards set a terrible example for their families and others. They become worse than usual and rush toward destruction. Don’t turn to alcohol when you’re down or tired. That just hurts you more and makes your problems worse.

Instead, pray earnestly so the Holy Spirit fills you and helps you avoid anything that might upset Him. God’s people have reasons to be joyful. Even though we don’t always feel like singing, we should always be thankful. We always have plenty of reasons to thank God throughout our lives.

Even during hard times, we should give thanks because we know everything happens for a purpose. God protects His followers from sinning against Him. He helps us obey His commands, honor Him, and fulfill our duties to each other.

5.o: The duty of wives is, submission to their husbands in the Lord, which includes honouring and obeying them, from a principle of love to them. The duty of husbands is to love their wives. The love of Christ to the church is an example, which is sincere, pure, and constant, notwithstanding her failures. Christ gave himself for the church, that he might sanctify it in this world, and glorify it in the next, that he might bestow on all his members a principle of holiness, and deliver them from the guilt, the pollution, and the dominion of sin, by those influences of the Holy Spirit, of which baptismal water was the outward sign. The church and believers will not be without spot or wrinkle till they come to glory. But those only who are sanctified now, shall be glorified hereafter. The words of Adam, mentioned by the apostle, are spoken literally of marriage; but they have also a hidden sense in them, relating to the union between Christ and his church. It was a kind of type, as having resemblance. There will be failures and defects on both sides, in the present state of human nature, yet this does not alter the relation. All the duties of marriage are included in unity and love. And while we adore and rejoice in the condescending love of Christ, let husbands and wives learn hence their duties to each other. Thus the worst evils would be prevented, and many painful effects would be avoided.
5.m:
Wives should submit to their husbands out of respect and love. This means honoring and obeying them. Husbands should love their wives. Jesus' love for the church shows us what true love looks like—it’s honest, clean, and lasts forever even if the church makes mistakes. Jesus died for the church so He could make it holy and free from sin. Baptism is a symbol of the Holy Spirit working inside people to help them become good. 

The church won’t be perfect until everyone gets to heaven. Only those who are made holy now will get to enjoy God’s glory later. When Paul talks about Adam’s words, he’s talking about marriage. But there’s more to it—Adam’s words also represent the relationship between Christ and His church. Marriage back then was meant to show something deeper. Even though people sometimes fail, the basic rules stay the same. Unity and love cover all the duties in marriage. As we praise God for Jesus’ humble love, let’s use it to guide our marriages. Then we’ll avoid many problems and keep things peaceful.

6.o: The great duty of children is, to obey their parents. That obedience includes inward reverence, as well as outward acts, and in every age prosperity has attended those distinguished for obedience to parents. The duty of parents. Be not impatient; use no unreasonable severities. Deal prudently and wisely with children; convince their judgements and work upon their reason. Bring them up well; under proper and compassionate correction; and in the knowledge of the duty God requires. Often is this duty neglected, even among professors of the gospel. Many set their children against religion; but this does not excuse the children's disobedience, though it may be awfully occasion it. God alone can change the heart, yet he gives his blessing to the good lessons and examples of parents, and answers their prayers. But those, whose chief anxiety is that their children should be rich and accomplished, whatever becomes of their souls, must not look for the blessing of God.
6.m:
Children need to obey their parents. This means showing respect inside and out. Throughout history, people who honored their parents have often been successful. Parents also have duties. They shouldn’t get angry too quickly or punish unfairly. Instead, they should teach their kids wisely by explaining things clearly. 

Parents should raise their children properly, using fair discipline and teaching them what God wants. Sadly, many families don't do this, especially some religious people. Some push their kids away from faith, which doesn’t excuse the kids' bad behavior. Only God can truly change hearts, but he blesses good parenting and listens to parents’ prayers. 

However, if parents only care about making their kids rich and famous without worrying about their spiritual lives, they won’t receive God’s blessings.

6.o: The duty of servants is summed up in one word, obedience. The servants of old were generally slaves. The apostles were to teach servants and masters their duties, in doing which evils would be lessened, till slavery should be rooted out by the influence of Christianity. Servants are to reverence those over them. They are to be sincere; not pretending obedience when they mean to disobey, but serving faithfully. And they must serve their masters not only when their master's eye is upon them; but must be strict in the discharge of their duty, when he is absent and out of the way. Steady regard to the Lord Jesus Christ will make men faithful and sincere in every station, not grudgingly or by constraint, but from a principle of love to the masters and their concerns. This makes service easy to them, pleasing to their masters, and acceptable to the Lord Christ. God will reward even the meanest drudgery done from a sense of duty, and with a view to glorify him. Here is the duty of masters. Act after the same manner. Be just to servants, as you expect they should be to you; show the like good-will and concern for them, and be careful herein to approve yourselves to God. Be not tyrannical and overbearing. You have a Master to obey, and you and they are but fellow-servants in respect to Christ Jesus. If masters and servants would consider their duties to God, and the account they must shortly give to him, they would be more mindful of their duty to each other, and thus families would be more orderly and happy.
6.m:
Servants should always follow orders. In ancient times, most servants were actually slaves. The apostles taught both servants and masters about their responsibilities. By following these rules, problems would decrease until slavery was removed thanks to Christian teachings.

Servants need to honor their bosses. They shouldn’t pretend to listen while planning to ignore commands. Instead, they should work honestly all the time, especially when their boss isn't watching. When people stay true to Jesus, they’ll act faithfully wherever they are. They won’t complain or feel forced, but do what’s right because they care about their bosses and their jobs. This makes working easier and pleases both the bosses and God.

God rewards everyone who works hard out of a desire to please Him, no matter how small the task. Masters have duties too. Treat your workers fairly, just as you want them to treat you. Show kindness and concern for them. Don’t be harsh or demanding. Remember, you have your own Master—Jesus—and you’re equal to your workers before Him. 

If bosses and workers think about their duties to God and remember they'll answer to Him soon, they’ll be kinder to each other. Then homes will run smoothly and everyone will be happier.

6.o: Spiritual strength and courage are needed for our spiritual warfare and suffering. Those who would prove themselves to have true grace, must aim at all grace; and put on the whole armour of God, which he prepares and bestows. The Christian armour is made to be worn; and there is no putting off our armour till we have done our warfare, and finished our course. The combat is not against human enemies, nor against our own corrupt nature only; we have to do with an enemy who has a thousand ways of beguiling unstable souls. The devils assault us in the things that belong to our souls, and labour to deface the heavenly image in our hearts. We must resolve by God's grace, not to yield to Satan. Resist him, and he will flee. If we give way, he will get ground. If we distrust either our cause, or our Leader, or our armour, we give him advantage. The different parts of the armour of heavy-armed soldiers, who had to sustain the fiercest assaults of the enemy, are here described. There is none for the back; nothing to defend those who turn back in the Christian warfare. Truth, or sincerity, is the girdle. This girds on all the other pieces of our armour, and is first mentioned. There can be no religion without sincerity. The righteousness of Christ, imputed to us, is a breastplate against the arrows of Divine wrath. The righteousness of Christ implanted in us, fortifies the heart against the attacks of Satan. Resolution must be as greaves, or armour to our legs; and to stand their ground or to march forward in rugged paths, the feet must be shod with the preparation of the gospel of peace. Motives to obedience, amidst trials, must be drawn from a clear knowledge of the gospel. Faith is all in all in an hour of temptation. Faith, as relying on unseen objects, receiving Christ and the benefits of redemption, and so deriving grace from him, is like a shield, a defence every way. The devil is the wicked one. Violent temptations, by which the soul is set on fire of hell, are darts Satan shoots at us. Also, hard thoughts of God, and as to ourselves. Faith applying the word of God and the grace of Christ, quenches the darts of temptation. Salvation must be our helmet. A good hope of salvation, a Scriptural expectation of victory, will purify the soul, and keep it from being defiled by Satan. To the Christian armed for defense in battle, the apostle recommends only one weapon of attack; but it is enough, the sword of the Spirit, which is the word of God. It subdues and mortifies evil desires and blasphemous thoughts as they rise within; and answers unbelief and error as they assault from without. A single text, well understood, and rightly applied, at once destroys a temptation or an objection, and subdues the most formidable adversary. Prayer must fasten all the other parts of our Christian armour. There are other duties of religion, and of our stations in the world, but we must keep up times of prayer. Though set and solemn prayer may not be seasonable when other duties are to be done, yet short pious prayers darted out, always are so. We must use holy thoughts in our ordinary course. A vain heart will be vain in prayer. We must pray with all kinds of prayer, public, private, and secret; social and solitary; solemn and sudden: with all the parts of prayer; confession of sin, petition for mercy, and thanksgiving for favours received. And we must do it by the grace of God the Holy Spirit, in dependence on, and according to, his teaching. We must preserve in particular requests, notwithstanding discouragements. We must pray, not for ourselves only, but for all saints. Our enemies are mighty, and we are without strength, but our Redeemer is almighty, and in the power of his mighty we may overcome. Wherefore we must stir up ourselves. Have not we, when God has called, often neglected to answer? Let us think upon these things, and continue our prayers with patience. (Eph 6:19-24)
6.m:
Spiritual strength and courage are important for fighting battles and enduring hardships. People who want to show they truly have God’s grace should work on developing all kinds of good qualities. They need to wear the full armor that God provides.

This armor is meant to be worn constantly until the end of our lives. Our fight isn’t just against people or our own weaknesses. It’s against evil spirits who try to trick us. These spirits attack our souls and try to ruin the goodness within us.

We must resist these spirits with God’s help. When we resist them, they run away. But if we give in, they gain power over us. Doubting God or our faith gives them an advantage.

The armor includes several pieces. First, truth or honesty ties everything together. Without honesty, there’s no real religion. 

Christ’s perfect righteousness protects us from God’s anger. His righteousness inside us guards our hearts from Satan’s attacks. 

Determination is like leg armor, helping us stay firm or move forward even when the path is tough. Understanding the gospel helps us walk bravely through trials.

Faith is key during times of testing. Faith trusts in what we can't see—like Jesus and his salvation. It acts like a shield protecting us from harmful thoughts and doubts.

Satan sends fiery temptations and harsh ideas about God and ourselves. Using the Word of God through faith stops these temptations. 

Salvation is our helmet, protecting our minds.

Having a strong belief in salvation and expecting victory over sin will clean our souls and protect them from Satan’s influence. For Christians who are ready to fight spiritually, the apostle suggests using just one weapon—the Word of God. This weapon can defeat bad desires and false ideas both inside and outside us. One verse used correctly can stop a temptation or solve a problem.

Prayer ties everything else together in our spiritual armor. Besides regular religious duties, we need to have times of prayer. Even if planned prayer sessions aren’t possible, quick, heartfelt prayers fit in anywhere. We should think about God during daily activities too. Vain hearts show in vain prayers.

We must pray in different ways—publicly, privately, secretly, alone or with others, formally or spontaneously. Prayers should include confessing sins, asking for help, and giving thanks. We must pray by God’s grace, following His guidance. 

In our specific requests, we shouldn’t give up even if faced with difficulties. We should pray not just for ourselves but for all believers. Our foes are powerful, but Christ is stronger than any enemy. With His might, we can win. So let’s stay motivated. Haven’t we sometimes ignored God’s call? Let’s remember this and keep praying patiently. (Eph 6:19-24)

6.o: The gospel was a mystery till made known by Divine revelation; and it is the work of Christ's ministers to declare it. The best and most eminent ministers need the prayers of believers. Those particularly should be prayed for, who are exposed to great hardships and perils in their work. Peace be to the brethren, and love with faith. By peace, understand all manner of peace; peace with God, peace of conscience, peace among themselves. And the grace of the Spirit, producing faith and love, and every grace. These he desires for those in whom they were already begun. And all grace and blessings come to the saints from God, through Jesus Christ our Lord. Grace, that is, the favour of God; and all good, spiritual and temporal, which is from it, is and shall be with all those who thus love our Lord Jesus Christ in sincerity, and with them only.
6.m:
The gospel was a secret until God revealed it. It’s the job of Christ’s followers to share it. Even the best leaders need people to pray for them. We especially should pray for those facing big challenges and dangers because of their work. 

May there be peace among the believers—peace with God, peace within, and peace between each other. May the Holy Spirit give them faith and love, along with all kinds of goodness. He wishes these things for those who already have some of them.

All grace and blessings come to God’s people through Jesus Christ. Grace means God’s favor, and all good things—both spiritual and physical—that flow from it—are given to those who truly love our Lord Jesus Christ.
